items.title: History of Urban Problems
items.source_text: Cities have been described as consisting of 'clusters of problems,' and this course aims to unpack some of those specific problems with an eye toward understanding the history of cities themselves. Students prepare research papers and presentations focusing on United States 19th-century examples, studying specific problems such as cholera epidemics, moral reform movements, the Great Chicago Fire, and urban transportation management.

items.title: The New Suburban History
items.source_text: To some, they represent single-family detached houses, manicured lawns and prosperity, epitomizing the 'American Dream.' To others, suburbs represent tacky conformity, racial segregation, class snobbery, and homogeneity. The course traces the historiographic evolution from the 'Old Suburban History' through newer scholarship emphasizing race, class diversity, environmental impact, and political conservatism in suburbia.

items.title: Race, Immigration & Urban Politics
items.source_text: New immigrants from the Caribbean, Latin America, Asia, and Europe have made the racial and ethnic composition of the already cosmopolitan city vastly more complex. The seminar examines how post-1965 immigration has transformed New York City's demographics, neighborhoods, economy, and politics, with students conducting hands-on research analyzing immigrant and native-born ethnic groups across education, employment, housing, and political participation.

items.title: Modern Central American History
items.source_text: This course covers Central American history from the dictators of the 1930s until the revolutionary decades and their aftermaths to understand historical continuities and ruptures. Topics include the Panama Canal, the Banana Wars and labor exploitation, guerrilla warfare, gendered resistance, and contemporary immigration crises rooted in Cold War-era violence.

items.title: Reform and Revolution in Latin America
topics: Cold War geopolitics in Latin America
items.source_text: This course focuses on reform movements and revolutions in modern Latin America as a way of considering how individuals and groups articulate their needs and demand access to resources, representation, and political change. Weekly topics span Peruvian reformism and APRA, the Mexican Revolution and 1917 Constitution, Guatemalan agrarian reform and US intervention, the Cuban Revolution, the Chilean road to socialism, the Sandinista Revolution, and Bolivian transformation under Evo Morales.

items.title: Colonial Latin American History
topics: transatlantic slave trade in Latin America
items.source_text: Latin America was born out of the complicated and often bloody encounter between Iberians (people from Spain and Portugal), Africans, and the indigenous peoples of the Americas. The course moves from Pre-Columbian Aztec and Inca societies through the Spanish conquest, colonial economy and disease impacts, the racial classification system of castas, Bourbon administrative reforms, and ultimately to the revolutionary independence movements of the early nineteenth century.
